speaker: interviewer
text: Seven interview with Mr. Harry Schlesinger. It is March the 30th, 1997. Mr. Schlesinger, you said there were one or two brief things you wanted to go back and mention before we finished up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Let me, please, to mention you the youngest survivor from Birkenau is one of my cousins. Who she is a daughter from my mother's brother's daughter. It means my mother brother, we used to live in Czechoslovakia. And he was sent to Theresienstadt, was also one of the camps. And he was married, and he had a baby from two years old. And because he had the guts to buy on the black market for the child an apple, for punishment he was sent to Auschwitz. I met my uncle there in Auschwitz while they gave him a shower. And he made briefly told me, Harry, there's not too much time to talk to you. All I tell you that I have here a child who is two year old. I have in my hand a little makorka. A mahorka is a tobak what has a value in the concentration camp. Sell this tobak and buy for my child a little toy and give it to her. Because, a little pleasure to give her. That's what I can do the last time in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked at him and he says, like, are you normal? I mean, all the children are dead. How can you even talk about it? He said, don't look at me like a crazy guy. I know my daughter is alive. She's within the woman camp, and she is alive. So what happened? She was a beautiful baby. And Mengele, while he made his selection, he picked her up from the mother's arm and gave it over to a Polish Gentile woman. She should take care of the baby. My sister, who is also a twin, she was standing every morning [INAUDIBLE]. They was counting the people. And this little two-year-old child was standing on her wrist with a tattoo number and standing like a regular grown up person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, after my sister became free in Auschwitz, she couldn't find her right away, but she knew here on her number, tattoo number, on her wrist. And she marked it down and came home. And  right away told the parents, Shloime, who is the baby's mother, who is my mother's brother, child is alive. This is the number. And somewhere in Poland has to be, because a Polish woman took care of her. They wrote letters to Switzerland, to the Red Cross. They wrote a letter to my uncle to Jerusalem who used to live in Israel. And he used to go to Switzerland for a visit, told her the whole story. They registered all over looking for her. Was no answer. One day they gave us said number in Poland between the nuns, we should send a letter, and ask the nuns if they could find the baby there. Because it is in Poland. And answer came back from Poland. Even if we could find the baby, and the baby was suffering already once in Poland, you don't want it should get the baby to a second problem. So even if we would find her, we wouldn't send it anyway back. This was the answer from the Polish nuns.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, how did we find out this baby, that she is alive after so many years? My mother, should rest in peace, who died in 1977, a year before we got the letter from our uncle, who is a sec-- my mother's second brother, younger brother in Israel. He in lives in Nahariya. And he calls up right away, [NON-ENGLISH] means the sister. They found Shloime's daughter. They found Shloime's daughter. How did you find it? He said, it happened like this. I never read Hungarian newspaper. Because there is in Israel a Hungarian newspaper. They call it Új Kelet. And we collect reporter met to Auschwitz to visit the Auschwitz museum. The manager from the museum-- who was a Polish guy-- showed him all kind of pictures and all kind of stories told them. And in between, he showed them a catalog where there are 36 children. All of them, they find their relatives. The only who didn't find their relative is Eva Kurtz, with three points. So this Hungarian newspaper man went back to Israel. And he wrote an article in the Hungarian newspaper. And the funny part, they wouldn't even look at this newspaper. And uncle wouldn't look it either. A new man, he opened up the newspaper. There are two pages. On one side, it says, Dr. Sternbach died, who was a big Zionist who was our uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the opposite side, right away, it says, Eva Kurtz, this and this number, is looking for the relatives. That's how we found her. Right away, we called up the HIAS. And the HIAS right away was ready to send a ticket and to bring her here to America. We wrote the letter right away, with a ticket together. And she wrote us back. This was- she were [INAUDIBLE] between 18 and 19 year old. And she wrote us back that she's already engaged to this boyfriend, and she can not come to America. He wouldn't let her. Because he was smart enough to know once she's here, she's here, she will stay here. Wasn't so good in Poland, she shouldn't stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: We ask her again, how come, for so many years, we didn't hear from you? I mean, if you was interested in your family? So give us the right explanation. She was adopted from this Polish woman who was married to a Polish man. And this Polish man husband did not give the right for the woman to tell the daughter that she is adopted daughter. And she was afraid from the husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once the husband died, by the time she became 18-year-old, she told her the truth. She didn't have to be afraid from the husband. And that's how we got in contact with her. She is today in Poland. We have her picture here. My mother want to convince herself and ask her, if you send me a pictures, please put your wrist in front. We should see your tattoo number. And right, this is the person. Then they heard in Israel about her. 22 mothers from Budapest, from Hungary, they wrote letters. You are my daughter. I want to have you. I'll do everything for you. But between-- and she contacted all the 22 [LAUGHS] ladies, and she got packages from her. And she was in contact with them. But none of the 22 could prove it that already in 1945, we were looking in Switzerland for her number. So actually, we are the real relatives. And he's- she really belongs to us. She lives today in Poland. She has two children. She brought up her children like Catholics and is still. So what I want to bring up, this is the youngest survivor what lives today in Poland under a Catholic name, and she lives there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you briefly mentioned you had one other thing that you wanted to mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About the Hungarians, when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in order to bring up the Hungarians, who they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's not get confused. Let's not hear stories that they were nice to us. They were worse than the Germans. They did a lot of dirty things with us. What should I tell them more? In a minute, I don't even think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, well, I had one final question--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you. You mentioned you came from a very, uh, a Hasidic background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are still an observant Jew today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, if at all, did your experiences in the different concentration camps affect your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do believe in God, yeah. I do believe in God. And there is a God. And we all should believe in it. Because a person who doesn't believe in a God, I consider him not even a human being. Between an animal and a human being, an animal is eating, a human being is eating. An animal is walking, a human being is walking. An animal is doing everything. The only thing what animal hasn't got, a belief. And if you don't believe, we are nothing other than animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would want to add in closing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to apologize when I made a statement about the Russian people. The Russian people are very nice people, good-natured people, warm people. Only their leader, who was Stalin, one of the biggest murderer, we could put him together with Hitler. Together. Not only that he was the worst murderer, in a way, he was even worse than Hitler, because he killed from his own people more than any other leader killed from the enemy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Russia is a nice country. The people are nice, and we hope we will one day make complete peace with them. But we should not forget Stalin's murders, what he did to his own people-- to the Jews and to his own Russian people. OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shall I start to talk already? Yeah. This picture is from my father's side, a wedding when my father's sister is getting married. You can see, if you're looking at it from the right side, the fourth person is a lady dressed up like a kallah girl. She's getting married and how the family got together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now let me point out who are those people on the picture. We start from the right to the left. The first person you see with a beard is my father's younger brother. Then next to see- to him is his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name is Herschel. Then you have my sister, who's the kallah, with Herman, and with her husband, I don't remember. Then you see my father after this, after the kallah. My father with his wife, it means my mother and my father. And the last two persons are my brother- my father's brother-in-law with his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: And their name-- [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then we start again from the right to the left. Again, my father's brother, who did not survive. He got killed in Russia like American who went to sweep the mines in Russia. Next to him is his wife. He's holding his baby, who went to Auschwitz, was this time maybe three or four year old. Then you see my great grandmother with my great grandfather, who's in the middle with the beard. This is my father's father. Next to it, you see my father's sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names, your grand grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Great grandfather is David Schlesinger, [NON-ENGLISH] Schlesinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his wife is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Hannah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hannah Schlesinger. Next to my grandfather is his daughter and the daughter's husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little boy who's standing is one of my cousin, whose parents are standing next to it, Mr. Weisel with his wife in the corner on the left side. Now the third row, we see here are the most famous twins, Harry and David Schlesinger. This is my sister. And over there, I am. Next to me is my cousin, who is brothers-- my father's brother's son. And other one is other cousin. The little cousin who is sitting next to my grandfather David Schlesinger, he survived with me together in Mauthausen. And he died after a few days. He became sick from eating the food, and he became sick. He died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all the details about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken roughly, I would say--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: This family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This family picture was taken also on a wedding occasion where my grandfather's from my mother's side, his brother's son got married. On the first line, they are all relatives from my grandfather's side from my mother's side. By the second line, when you see an older person with a white beard, he is my mother's father. His name is Yankel Stern. And most of the family is all-- and then you see on the third line also a person who looks like my grandfather. In fact, they look like twins also with the white beard, this is his brother. So the rest of the people are all from his side of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandfather's brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schmiel Stern. And he was Yankel Stern. Now when you see here under the line where my grandfather is, is my uncle, who used to live in Naharyia, whom I was mentioning. He discovered this twin from the newspaper. And he's standing there. And this is the first under my grandfather's picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rest are- the rest are all from my mother's grandfather's side the family. From all the pictures what you see here, roughly speaking, about six or seven people are alive. They all died in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this also taken in Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Munkacs? Yeah. Yeah. This picture was taken in Munkacs. This is my father's-- should rest in peace-- his jewelery store where he was working over 25 years. In a nice day, they came in, the Germans, the Hungarians, and they took it away from him. You are Jewish. You have no right to have it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how we lost the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture and the other pictures from before the war, how did you get these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the people who are Gentiles, they found it in a certain place and they return it to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken next to the Polish border. They call the place [NON-ENGLISH] where my uncle, Dr. Sternbach who used to live in Jerusalem, was a very big scientist and a good-natured person. His parents used to live in this little place, as I told you, next to the border from Poland, between Hungary and Poland. He had a little grocery store. And because his parents were always alone, the children had already left the father and mother, he asked my mother that to make a big favor and every summer, when you go for summer place, don't go to another place. Just go to [NON-ENGLISH]. At least my parents would have the pleasure to be the children and with you together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here you see on the left side my mother. My mother is holding me-- this is Harry. And the next to it, my sister, the twin sister, and one of the relatives from the family. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in a Hebrew elementary school when we were in the first grade. I, myself, am on the second line. The second line from the right side, the fourth person. And my sister is on the left side from the last line, the sixth person. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This picture was taken for my family. My father, my sister, I, myself, and my mother in 1946 when we came back from concentration camp. We were really lucky. Something unusual, with the whole Munkacs, was two complete family came home. And we were one of the two families. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This paper is an identification from Mauthausen when I became free through the American army. In order nobody to deny that ever happened at Mauthausen. Not only I am the witness to it, the paper is a witness. And the American army is always a witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a copy?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a copy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have the original?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the original, too. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This picture is for myself, was taken in 1946 in Czechoslovakia. This picture was taken here in America. Here I am at a few years older as you can see on the picture. Here is my lovely wife, I, myself, and my two lovely sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna Schlesinger. And the one to the right is David Schlesinger. And the one to the left is William Schlesinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here are, again, my two lovely sons, David and William Schlesinger. David is on the left side, and William is on the right side

speaker: interviewer
text: And roughly when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken approximately about five or six years ago, about '92, '93.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
